article_title: Apple Plans to Replace Core ML with Core AI Framework at WWDC 2026, Signaling a Developer-Facing AI Platform Shift

content_preview.title: Apple Plans to Replace Core ML with Core AI Framework at WWDC 2026, Signaling a Developer-Facing AI Platform Shift
content_preview.summary: Apple is reportedly planning to retire Core ML at WWDC 2026 and introduce Core AI, a modernized framework intended to help developers integrate outside AI models into iOS apps, according to Bloomberg's Mark Gurman.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Apple is reportedly preparing to retire Core ML, the machine learning framework it introduced in 2017, and replace it with a new system called Core AI at its Worldwide Developers Conference in June 2026, according to Bloomberg's Mark Gurman, as reported by [9to5Mac](https://9to5mac.com/2026/03/01/apple-replacing-core-ml-with-modernized-core-ai-framework-for-ios-27-at-wwdc/). The planned transition, expected to accompany iOS 27, would mark a structural change in how Apple exposes art...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: The rewrite is substantially improved. The article now presents the Core AI story clearly as a Bloomberg/Gurman report — not a confirmed Apple announcement — with appropriate hedging throughout. Both direct Gurman quotes are retained and properly attributed via 9to5Mac. The 'Context and Caveats' section explicitly flags the single-source nature of the reporting. Word count of 589 is within the News category range (400–1200). The article is well-structured with a logical flow from framework history to developer implications to broader AI strategy.
editor_notes.source_verification: 9to5Mac (https://9to5mac.com/2026/03/01/apple-replacing-core-ml-with-modernized-core-ai-framework-for-ios-27-at-wwdc/): VERIFIED. Confirmed accessible and correctly cited as the outlet reporting Gurman's Power On newsletter. Both quoted passages ('planning a few other software-based AI upgrades, including a new framework called Core AI' and 'The switch from ML to AI is significant...') are confirmed present in this article. No mention of MCP in source. MacRumors (https://www.macrumors.com/2026/01/25/siri-google-gemini-release-date/): VERIFIED. Confirmed accessible. Reports Siri/Gemini integration, iOS 26.4 and iOS 27 Siri overhaul details, attributed to Bloomberg/Gurman. The article's claims about iOS 26.4 and iOS 27 Siri improvements are directly supported by this source. iLounge (https://www.ilounge.com/news/apple-moving-to-core-ai-replacing-core-ml-in-a-wwdc-2026-reveal): VERIFIED accessible. Confirmed no MCP mention — critical hallucination from round 1 has been removed. iLounge is used narrowly in one sentence to note the transition 'addresses the expanded scope of what developers are building' — a reasonable paraphrase of its general framing. No unsourced technical specifics are attributed to it.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All specific claims in the article are traceable to verified sources. The MCP hallucination (round 1 critical finding) has been fully removed. The AppleMagazine-sourced speculative technical claims (multimodal inputs, Apple Foundation Models integration, iPhone 17 Pro neural engine specifics) have been removed. The article now explicitly notes that 'specific technical details about supported model types, input modalities, or integration protocols have not been reported by primary sources' — an honest and accurate qualification. The Siri/Gemini section accurately represents what MacRumors reported.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral throughout. No sensationalism. No editorializing beyond the final paragraph's measured synthesis, which is clearly framed as conditional ('if Apple executes it as described'). No AI self-reference. The article appropriately distinguishes between what Gurman reported and what remains unconfirmed.
editor_notes.originality: No duplicate published article found in src/content/articles/ for this topic. The story is original to this publication.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: All three critical and significant findings from round 1 have been correctly addressed: the MCP/iLounge fabricated attribution has been removed, the summary now uses appropriate hedging language, and AppleMagazine with its unsourced technical claims has been dropped entirely. The rewritten article is factually sound, properly hedged, and well-sourced. The allowlist warnings for MacRumors and iLounge are minor — both are legitimate Apple news outlets and the allowlist is a configuration matter, not a journalistic concern. The article is ready for publication.
